{"step_by_step_thinking": "The patient presents with right upper quadrant pain, nausea, fever (temperature 38.5°C), leukocytosis (WBC 14,000/mm3), and right upper quadrant tenderness. These findings are consistent with biliary pathology. The laboratory results show mild elevations in AST and ALT, but normal total bilirubin, alkaline phosphatase, and normal liver enzymes suggest no significant biliary obstruction or cholestasis. Cholescintigraphy shows that the intrahepatic bile ducts, hepatic ducts, common bile duct, and proximal small bowel are patent, indicating no obstruction in these structures. This rules out a common bile duct obstruction or distal biliary obstruction. The absence of jaundice (normal bilirubin) and the presence of leukocytosis point toward inflammation rather than obstruction. The most common cause of right upper quadrant pain with leukocytosis and tenderness is acute cholecystitis, which is typically due to obstruction of the cystic duct by a gallstone. This obstruction leads to inflammation and infection, resulting in pain, fever, and leukocytosis. Although the ultrasound was inconclusive, cholescintigraphy showing normal flow through the common bile duct suggests the obstruction is likely proximal, such as in the cystic duct. Option D, obstruction of the cystic duct, is the most likely cause. Option A (autodigestion of pancreatic parenchyma) would present with epigastric pain radiating to the back, elevated lipase, and is less likely given the location and lab findings. Option B (fistula between gallbladder and small intestine) is rare and would typically present with more complex symptoms like obstruction or fistula-related complications, not typical of this presentation. Option C (hepatotropic virus) would likely present with more significant liver enzyme elevations and jaundice, which are not seen here. Therefore, the most likely cause is obstruction of the cystic duct.", "answer_choice": "D"}
